journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
title: ATP2B1 expression identifies human hematopoietic stem cells with superior repopulation and self-renewal
abstract: Long-term hematopoietic stem cells (LT-HSC) maintain lifelong hematopoiesis while preserving the stem cell compartment through self-renewal. The human LT-HSC compartment is molecularly and functionally heterogeneous and also varies across ontogeny. Dissecting the molecular basis for this variation is impeded by the paucity of immunophenotypic markers to resolve LT-HSC heterogeneity. Here, we identified ATPase plasma membrane calcium transporting 1 (ATP2B1/PMCA1) as a cell surface marker that is heterogeneously expressed by CD49f+ LT-HSC from fetal to adult hematopoiesis. ATP2B1 immunophenotypic expression stratified human CD49f+ LT-HSC from fetal liver, neonatal cord blood, and adult mobilized peripheral blood sources into functionally distinct subpopulations in single-cell (sc) clonogenic assays. CD49f+ATP2B1+ LT-HSC exhibited superior long-term repopulation and self-renewal capacities in vivo compared to CD49f+ATP2B1– LT-HSC. Molecular profiling by scMultiome and immunofluorescence microscopy point to enrichment of an HSC self-renewal program that includes the TFEB–endolysosomal axis in CD49f+ATP2B1+ LT-HSC. Our study provides a framework to dissect the heterogeneous molecular programs in LT-HSC.
discussion: DiscussionHere, we demonstrate that ATP2B1 is expressed on the surface of human LT-HSC in FL and CB, and modestly present on adult mPB and BM LT-HSC. Similar to previously described markers, ATP2B1 surface staining identifies a single continuous HSC population rather than discrete subsets. Nonetheless, ATP2B1 expression bifurcates the widely used gold standard marker CD49f into two populations with distinct functional properties. When compared to CD49f+ATP2B1– HSC, the CD49f+ATP2B1+ subset had higher serial clonogenic progenitor replating and better repopulation and self-renewal in xenografts. ATP2B1 expression and serial clonogenic output were both much higher in LT-HSC from human FL followed by a gradual reduction in CB and mPB; the latter finding is consistent with prior studies using less purified CD34+ subsets . While ATP2B1 expression is heterogeneous across the human lifespan, our data suggest its expression is reflective of functional stemness programs with demonstration of unit purity in transplantation using CB HSC. Thus, interrogation of CD49f+ATP2B1+ HSC across ontogeny should reveal both common stemness programs along with those that change from fetal to adult HSC states.Recent scRNAseq of HSPCs across the human lifespan have uncovered dynamic changes associated with human blood progenitors from fetal development through adulthood . These studies reinforced the importance of integrating transcriptional and functional features of HSPCs at different developmental stages , but still did not achieve enough resolution to capture specific facets of LT-HSC biology. Our study dissects both the molecular and functional properties of human CD90+ HSC-derived subsets at single-cell resolution. scMultiome profiling identified TFEB-mediated control of catabolic processes as one of the molecular mechanisms underpinning the distinct stemness advantage of CD49f+ATP2B1+ HSC. These findings refine our prior studies that linked TFEB activity to LT-HSC vs. ST-HSC by showing that TFEB and lysosomal activity preserved quiescence and enhanced self-renewal of CB LT-HSC . Our work solidifies the value of quantifying the nuclear-versus-non-nuclear localization of TFEB—in addition to its total content—as an extra layer of molecular information that helps identify LT-HSC with superior functionality across a spectrum of HSC sources .Our study suggests a link between ATP2B1 surface expression and LT-HSC-specific endolysosomal programs and calcium homeostasis, pinpointing particular pathways regulating HSC functionality in the human setting. Autophagy is a catabolic process, centered at the lysosome, directly implicated in HSC dormancy and self-renewal . Further, recent studies point to metabolic and inflammatory stress programs contributing to the functional attrition of HSC with human development , though many functionally young HSC can persist into old age . Murine HSC are found in niches with elevated calcium . Although autophagy is decreased overall in murine HSC during aging, a subset of adult HSC activates autophagy to counteract stress- and inflammation-associated metabolic impairment . While we have not directly interrogated autophagy flux in this study, we hypothesize that the ATP2B1+CD49f+ HSC we have identified in humans are the ortholog of the aforementioned autophagy-activated, stress-resistant HSC, which perhaps function by preserving calcium homeostasis through development—and potentially pinpointing why ATP2B1+CD49f+ numbers are decreased by ~threefold in mPB as compared to CB. Future studies employing robust low-input-to-single-cell metabolomic profiling to directly measure metabolites in ATP2B1+ and ATP2B1– LT-HSC should shed considerable light on human HSC heterogeneity . Overall, our findings serve as a foundation to accelerate a deeper understanding of the molecular and metabolic programs governing human LT-HSC across multiple stem cell sources.Limitations of the Study.In our study, we demonstrate that ATP2B1 surface expression allows purification of functionally distinct human LT-HSC across ontogeny. We relied on CB as being representative for the gold-standard xenotransplantation assays as well as scMultiomics, as FL, mPB, and BM are limited resources. Concerning xenotransplantation assays, our conclusions are limited to HSC transplanted by intrafemoral injection, and further work is required to ascertain the homing capacity of ATP2B1-expressing LT-HSC relative to their counterparts that lack expression. We also elected to utilize SGM3 mice to determine primary SRC frequency, due to their enhanced sensitivity of HSC detection. However, a direct comparison between SGM3 and NSG mice needs to be done. On the mechanistic side, we have not determined if the expression of ATP2B1 is sufficient to endow stemness properties onto human HSPC. Any genetic perturbation of ATP2B1 would require in vitro culture which has been shown to trigger immune-mediated inflammatory pathways and genetic aberrations not observed at homeostasis . Furthermore, overexpressing ATP2B1 in primary cells is challenging owing to the larger size of the ATP2B1 transgene (>3.5 kb). ATP2B1 also has multiple transcript variants, and we have not been able to isolate which are expressed in HSC or important for the phenotypes observed in this study, subsequently making Cas9-gRNA-mediated targeting a significant challenge. To overcome some of these hurdles, we functionally validated the role of ATP2B1 in our assays through high and low calcium culture and by using the selective small molecule inhibitor PI-8 in unperturbed settings. Despite our data suggesting that ATP2B1 expression and the endolysosomal pathways are connected, future mechanistic studies are needed to investigate this hypothesis and underpin the complex mechanism endowing CD49f+ATP2B1+ HSC with overall superior function and resilience to culture in high calcium.
figure_legends: ATP2B1 is expressed on the surface of human LT-HSC across ontogeny. (A and B): Representative images (A) and quantification (B) of confocal microscopy images of LT-HSC and ST-HSC stained for ATP2B1 and DAPI. (Scale bar, 2 μm); n = 2 CB, 195 individual cells/slide. Scattered dot plot shows ATP2B1-stained area in individual cells, with a line at the Median. A Mann–Whitney test was used to compare medians. (C) Representative flow cytometry plots showing ATP2B1 and CD49f expression and population gates within human CD34+CD38–CD45RA–CD90+ HSC in FL, CB, mPB, and BM. Light blue: FMO: Fluorescent Minus One; red: ATP2B1 stained cells. (D) Percentage of ATP2B1+ cells within CD49f+ LT-HSC in n = 4 FL, n = 10 CB, n = 10 mPB, and n = 6 BM. Bars represent means of values from individual donors shown as dots, error bars denote SD. Symbols of mPB and BM denote age: triangles (<45 y), rhombi (45 to 60 y), and circles (>60 y). A one-way ANOVA with Dunnett’s multiple comparison test was used to evaluate differences. (E) The shift in mean fluorescence intensity (MFI shift) for ATP2B1 in LT-HSC and ST-HSC from flow cytometry in (D); ratio-paired t tests were used to compare expression between the two populations. Symbols & lines reflect matched values from individual donors.

ATP2B1+ LT-HSC have superior in vitro clonogenic potential. (A–D) CD49f+ATP2B1+, CD49f+ATP2B1– and CD49f–ATP2B1– clonogenic assays showing number of primary colonies/100 seeded HSC (A); percentage of CD34+ within CD45+ cells (B) from primary colonies in (A); number of secondary colonies/100 HSC originally seeded for primary plating (C); percentage of CD34+ within CD45+ cells (D) from secondary colonies in (C); n = 5 FL, n = 14 CB, n =1 0 mPB. A one-way ANOVA with Holm–Šídák multiple comparison test was used to compare means, represented by bars; individual values are shown as dots. Error bars show SEM. (E) Proportions of lineage output from in vitro single-cell differentiation assays of HSC from FL (n = 2; 699 cloned cells), CB (n = 3; 591 cloned cells), and mPB (n = 4; 1,508 cloned cells). Independent donors were used for FL and mPB, and donor pools used for CB. Stacked bars show mean proportions; CD34+: CD34+ only colonies, E = erythroid (CD45–/GlyA+), M = myeloid (CD14+ and/or CD66+ and/or CD33+), Mk = megakaryocytic (CD41+), NK: CD56+ cells. (F) Percentage of clones containing CD34+ cells in single-cell in vitro differentiation assays in (E). Bars represent means of individual values shown as dots, and error bars show SEM. A Kruskal–Wallis test with Dunn’s correction was used to compare groups. (G) The number of CD34+ cells in each clone in the single-cell in vitro differentiation assays shown in (E). Scatter dot plots representing means of individual values shown as dots and error bars show mean with SEM, with dots representing individual values. The y-axis is logarithmic. (H) Percentage of single cells that grew into colonies containing one (Monopotent), two (Bipotent), or three or more (Multipotent) lineages in the in vitro differentiation assays in (E). Stacked bars show mean proportion, error bars show SD. (I–L) Primary (I and K) and secondary (J and L) colony forming assays using CD49f+ATP2B1+, CD45RA–CD49f+EPCR+, CD49f+CD112lo, CD49f+CLEC9A2hiCD34lo, and CD34+ CD38– GPRC5C+; the number of primary (I) and secondary (J) colonies relative to CD49f+ATP2B1+ are shown. A one-way ANOVA with a Holm–Šídák multiple comparison test was used to compare means, represented by bars; individual values (n = 6 CB) are shown as dots, and error bars show SEM. The %CD34 within CD45+ cells in the colonies from the primary (K) or secondary (L) plating are shown for individual donors. Data are shown and compared as in (I and J). (M–P) Ratio of colony counts/100 HSC originally seeded for primary plating (M and N) and ratio of %CD34+ within CD45+ cells (O and P) from clonogenic assays of CD49f+ATP2B1+ and CD49f+ATP2B1– HSC after primary (M and O) and secondary (N and P) plating in presence of Ctrl or 1 μM PI-8 (n = 4 FL, n = 6 CB, n = 3 mPB). Bars show means of individual values shown as dots, and error bars show SEM. A one-way ANOVA with Šidák’s multiple comparison test was used to compare groups.

CD49f+ATP2B1+ LT-HSC are enriched for TFEB-regulated lysosome-associated programs. (A and B) Distribution of Z scores reflecting enrichment of the LT/HSPC epigenetic signature (A) and the qHSC vs. aHSC transcriptional signature (B) in CD49f+ATP2B1+, CD49f+ATP2B1– and CD49f–ATP2B1– cells profiled by scMultiome. A Kruskal–Wallis test with Dunn’s correction was used to compare distributions. Box and whiskers show median values with minimum and maximum values. (C) GSEA results depicting enrichment of stem cell signatures across CD49f+ATP2B1+, CD49f+ATP2B1– and CD49f–ATP2B1– HSC. (D–F) Distribution of Z scores reflecting enrichment of the L-NMF16 (D), L-NMF6 (E), and L-NMF23 signatures (F) in HSC profiled by scMultiome. Data are presented and compared as in (A and B). (G) Unique Gene ontology (GO) terms enriched by GSEA with a Q value <0.05 within top driver genes from the L-NMF16 signature; (Top) GO biological processes (BP) terms; (Bottom) GO cellular component (CC) terms. The dashed red line indicates Q value threshold of <0.05. Bars indicate the negative logarithm to base 10 of the Q values for the indicated processes. (H) Predicted GO BP terms enriched in the output from a cis-regulatory region function analysis (GREAT) of the uniquely accessible chromatin peaks in CD49f+ATP2B1+ cells profiled by scMultiome. The dashed red line indicates the set significance threshold of Q <0.05. Bars indicate the negative logarithm to base 10 of the Q values for the indicated processes. (I) Representative images (Bottom) and quantification (Top) of confocal microscopy of individual CD49f+ATP2B1+, CD49f+ATP2B1– and CD49f–ATP2B1– HSC stained for TFEB (red) and DAPI (blue). (Scale bar, 2 μm); n = 3 FL, 352 cells, n = 3 CB, 377 cells, and n = 3 mPB, 422 cells. Quantification from individual cells is shown as dots, and the median shown with a horizontal line. A Kruskal–Wallis test with Dunn’s multiple comparison test was used to compare distributions. (J) Ratio of LysoTracker staining after culture in high vs. low calcium (Ca) of CD49f+ATP2B1+, CD49f+ATP2B1– and CD49f–ATP2B1– HSC; MFI for each replicate (n = 4 CB) in high Ca is normalized to the matching MFI from low Ca. (K) Distribution of CD34+CD90+CD45RA– (cLT), CD34+CD90–CD45RA– (cST) and CD34+CD90–CD45RA+ (cProg) within cultured CD49f+ATP2B1+, CD49f+ATP2B1– and CD49f–ATP2B1– HSC after 14 d in high vs. low Ca (n = 5). Bars show the median and error bars show the interquartile range. A Geisser–Greenhouse-corrected two-way ANOVA with Dunnett’s multiple comparison against CD49f+ATP2B1+ in low Ca was performed to compare values. (L) Quantification of ratio of nuclear/non-nuclear CDK6 from individual cells is shown as dots; the median is shown with a horizontal line. A Kruskal–Wallis test with Dunn’s multiple comparison test was used to compare distributions. n = 3 FL, 367 cells, n = 3 CB, 417 cells, and n = 3 mPB, 349 cells. Representative images from confocal microscopy of individual CB-derived CD49f+ATP2B1+, CD49f+ATP2B1– and CD49f–ATP2B1– HSC stained for CDK6 (red) and DAPI (blue) are shown to the right.

CD49f+ATP2B1+ LT-HSC display superior in vivo repopulation capacity and self-renewal. (A) Schematic for xenograft assays; CD49f+ATP2B1+, CD49f+ATP2B1– and CD49f–ATP2B1– cells were sorted from n = 4 CB pools and injected into conditioned NSG mice (~2,500 cells/mouse, 4 to 5 mice/group). Human chimerism was analyzed by flow cytometry at 4w or 20w after xenotransplantation. (B–E) %CD45+ human cells (B and D) and %CD34+CD19– cells (C and E) in injected femurs of xenografted mice at 4w (B and C) and 20w (D and E) postengraftment. Pairwise Mann–Whitney tests were used to compare median engraftment, shown as the line in the box and whisker plots. Error bars show the range of the individual values from each mouse, shown as dots. (F and G) Schematic (F) for secondary transplants with limiting dilution assays (LDA). hCD45+ cells from bone marrow of primary transplanted NSG mice at 20w (A, D, and E) were sorted and injected into NSG-SGM3 recipients at limiting doses: details are in (G). Bone marrow was analyzed for human chimerism by flow cytometry after 8w. (H) Graphical summary of the LDA showing HSC frequencies within the three biological replicates of primary grafts; a chi-squared test was performed to evaluate differences. (I and J) Number of total human CD45+ cells in injected femur (I) and noninjected bones (J) after 8w of secondary engraftment in NSG-SGM3 mice. Points reflect engraftment in individual mice, and a horizontal line reflects the median, compared using Mann–Whitney tests; error bars show the interquartile range. The line denotes the average number of human CD45 that reflects a graft of 0.05% of live cells across all mice. (K and L) Schematic (K) and summary (L) of a primary LDA for CD49f+ATP2B1+ and CD49f+ATP2B1– HSC. Cells were sorted from a mix of eight CB donors and injected into irradiated NSG-SGM3 recipients at limiting doses. Human chimerism was analyzed by flow cytometry at 8w after xenotransplantation. (M) Graphical summary of the primary LDA showing HSC frequencies within grafts; a chi-squared test was performed to evaluate differences. (N and O) Engraftment in NSG-SGM3 mice as %hCD45+ cells in injected (N) and noninjected (O) femurs after 8w.
